name: African Blue Lily
description: African Blue Lily (Agapanthus) is often mistaken for a true lily, but it belongs to a different plant family and does not cause the acute kidney failure associated with true lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis species). However, it is mildly toxic to cats and can cause gastrointestinal distress when any part of the plant — including the leaves, stems, or roots — is ingested. Cats are sometimes attracted to its grass-like foliage, so households with this plant should monitor access.
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, and excessive drooling are the most common signs of ingestion.
Not a true lily: Unlike Lilium or Hemerocallis species, this plant does not cause kidney failure, making it significantly less dangerous — but it is still not safe to ingest.
Action: Monitor your cat after any suspected ingestion; contact a vet if symptoms persist or worsen.

symptoms.name: Immediate intense pain and local irritation to mucous membranes
symptoms.notes: This is caused by insoluble calcium oxalate crystals found in all parts of the plant (especially the rhizomes) and a sticky, acrid latex. The pain usually limits the amount a cat will swallow3. 2. Excessive Drooling (Hypersalivation)

symptoms.name: Excess salivation
symptoms.notes: This occurs as a physiological response to the mechanical irritation and burning sensation in the mouth3. 3. Swelling of the Mouth and Throat

symptoms.name: Swollen tongue and pharynx (throat)
symptoms.notes: The inflammation is a direct result of the calcium oxalate crystals embedding in the soft tissues3. 4. Difficulty Breathing (Dyspnea)

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.notes: Breathing difficulties arise if the swelling of the tongue and pharynx becomes significant enough to obstruct the airway3. 5. Gastrointestinal Upset

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: The plant contains an irritant latex that disrupts the GI tract. Unlike true lilies, it does not cause acute renal failure
